claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic null-suffix appended to a smaller core vocabulary: each base word receives one of a small set of suffixes (e.g., -y, -in, -ain, -dy, -edy) to produce surface tokens, meaning the effective vocabulary after stripping common suffixes would shrink to roughly 2,000â€“2,500 unique roots with a Zipf exponent closer to 1.0.
evidence_for: Top words 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy', 'chol', 'chey' share recurring suffix clusters (-ain, -edy, -ol, -ey)
Word-final glyph distribution strongly concentrated on y, n, l, r, o â€” consistent with a small suffix inventory
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is flatter than natural language (~1.0), consistent with vocabulary inflation by suffix variation
High hapax ratio is more naturally explained by combinatorial suffix attachment than by rich natural-language vocabulary
evidence_against: If suffixes were purely null/decorative, word-internal glyph entropy should drop after stripping â€” not yet verified
Currier A/B split may reflect suffix-set differences, complicating a uniform stripping rule
Some hapax forms may be genuine rare words rather than suffix variants
test: Enumerate candidate suffix families by clustering word-final bigrams and trigrams; strip the top 10 suffix patterns from all tokens; recompute vocabulary size, hapax ratio, and Zipf exponent on the stemmed corpus.
test_metric: Post-stripping hapax ratio and Zipf exponent
test_threshold: Confirm if post-stripping hapax ratio falls below 40% and Zipf exponent rises above 0.95; deny if hapax ratio remains above 60% or Zipf exponent does not change significantly.

tests_run.test: enumerate candidate suffix families by clustering word-final bigrams and trigrams; strip the top 10
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If a handful of suffixes account for the vocabulary explosion, stripping them would reveal a compact core vocabulary compatible with a known medieval language, dramatically narrowing the decipherment search space.
